coredata.dc:title: One-step preparation of N, S-doped graphene quantum dots for white light-emitting diodes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xinghao
Li, Nan
Lv, Xugui
Wang, Jingjing
Ma, Qingyu
Xie, Zheng
coredata.dc:description: Two-dimensional (2D) materials consist of either a single layer or just a few layers of atoms or molecular layers, and their exceptional optoelectronic properties including high carrier mobility, wide spectral absorption range, and optical response from the number of layers. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a small-sized 2D material exhibited unique physical and chemical properties at the quantum scale. In this study, the N, S-doped GQDs (named as Y-GQDs) were synthesized through straightforward solvothermal process using 2, 2-dithiobenzoic acid and o-phenylenediamine as the precursors. The distribution of the prepared GQDs sample was uniform, with height varying between 0.6 and 0.8 nm. The excellent optical properties of Y-GQDs were attributed to the high crystalline graphene structure and rich functional groups including -C-SO3-C-, -C-SO4-C-, C-N, etc. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of Y-GQDs were 380 nm and 535 nm, respectively. Additionally, the multi-color and tunable luminescence characteristics could be achieved by excitation wavelength, solvent, and concentration. Gratifyingly, the N, S-doped GQDs were successfully used to build white light emitting devices via a sol-gel process. The color rendering index reached 83.7 with color temperature of 5306 K, and the GQDs exhibited good optical stability under different driving currents and UV light. This study underscored the significant potential of GQDs in creating promising phosphors for superior lighting applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
Heteroatom doping
Excitation & solution-dependent
White light-emitting diodes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) HRTEM images and (c) corresponding SAED pattern of prepared Y-GQDs sample; (d) diameter distribution of Y-GQDs particles; (e) AFM images; (f) Thickness distribution map; (g) XRD patterns; (h) Raman spectrum of Y-GQDs.
The FTIR spectrum (a); (b) XPS spectra; (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) O 1s, (f) S 2p high-resolution XPS spectra of Y-GQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of Y-GQDs; (b) CIE coordinates of Y-GQDs; (c) Fluorescence intensity curve of Y-GQDs under continuous ultraviolet lamp irradiation for 1 h; (d) Fluorescence intensity curves of Y-GQDs at room temperature for 30 days.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of Y-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths; (b) CIE coordinates; (c) Normalized fluorescence spectra; (d) Fluorescence lifetime fitting.

Y-GQDs in different solvents (a) Fluorescence spectra under ultraviolet light; (b) UV–visible absorption spectra under daylight; (c) Normalized fluorescence spectra; (d) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra; (e) Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra; (f) Fluorescence spectra; (g) Normalized fluorescence spectra at low concentrations; (h) Normalized fluorescence spectra at high concentrations.

(a) Electroluminescence spectrum of WLED, illustrated by a photograph of WLED; (b) CIE coordinates; (c) Electroluminescence spectra of WLED at different driving current; (d) color rendering index (CRI) and color temperature (CCT) change curves.
The performance of parameters of WLED under different driving current.
Comparison of luminescence performance characteristics of CDs/GQDs and their modified for fabrication of WLEDs.

head.ce:title: One-step preparation of N, S-doped graphene quantum dots for white light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Conversion and Optoelectronic Materials, Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two-dimensional (2D) materials consist of either a single layer or just a few layers of atoms or molecular layers, and their exceptional optoelectronic properties including high carrier mobility, wide spectral absorption range, and optical response from the number of layers. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a small-sized 2D material exhibited unique physical and chemical properties at the quantum scale. In this study, the N, S-doped GQDs (named as Y-GQDs) were synthesized through straightforward solvothermal process using 2, 2-dithiobenzoic acid and o-phenylenediamine as the precursors. The distribution of the prepared GQDs sample was uniform, with height varying between 0.6 and 0.8 nm. The excellent optical properties of Y-GQDs were attributed to the high crystalline graphene structure and rich functional groups including -C-SO3-C-, -C-SO4-C-, C-N, etc. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of Y-GQDs were 380 nm and 535 nm, respectively. Additionally, the multi-color and tunable luminescence characteristics could be achieved by excitation wavelength, solvent, and concentration. Gratifyingly, the N, S-doped GQDs were successfully used to build white light emitting devices via a sol-gel process. The color rendering index reached 83.7 with color temperature of 5306 K, and the GQDs exhibited good optical stability under different driving currents and UV light. This study underscored the significant potential of GQDs in creating promising phosphors for superior lighting applications.

ce:section.ce:para:
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were a kind of graphene fragments consisting of two dimensional (2D) network with sp
2
- and sp
3
-bonded atoms, and belonged to carbon based semiconductor nanocrystals [1,2]. The GQDs maintained the layered structure that was typical of graphene while exhibiting quantum confinement effects, distinguishing them from other types of CDs that lacked these characteristics. Because of the quantum size effect, the GQDs with tunable interfacial structures showed unique properties including biological low toxicity, chemical inertia, good biocompatibility, and excellent luminous performance. Hence they were widely used in the fields of sensor, catalysis, biomedicine, and optoelectronics [3,4].

To further boost the application and practicality of GQDs, methods such as heteroatom doping, surface modification, and material composite were employed [5]. The strategy of heteroatom doping was proven effective in enhancing the quantum yield, light stability, emission wavelength, and other properties. For example, due to their similar sizes but significantly different electronegativity, introducing N atoms into GQDs could effectively alter the tunable photoluminescence-related properties [6]. Tang et al. [7] used ammonia water and glucose solution as the precursors via mutual adsorption polymerization between amino functional groups to prepare N-doped GQDs. And results showed that optical properties were greatly improved after N doping. S-doped GQDs with tunable electronic structures and optical properties were synthesized through a hydrothermal process, where fructose was used as the carbon source in H2SO4 and reacted at 170°C for 4 h [8]. Furthermore, the co-doped GQDs had been widely explored. The N, S co-doped GQDs were prepared by Cheng et al. [9] via hydrothermal method using citric acid, sodium hydrosulfide, and O-phenylenediamine as precursors. The resulting GQDs demonstrated dual functionality as light-emitting diode emitters and photo-catalysts for hydrogen evolution. Shen et al. [10] used 1,3,6-Trinitropyrene and thiourea as precursors to synthesis of N, S co-doped GQDs with highly luminescent, and the co-doped GQDs were able to differentially detect metal ions in a mixture. N, S co-doped GQDs, synthesized via diverse methods and precursors, had been widely utilized in multiple fields, including solid-state luminescence [11], bio-imaging [12], solar cells [13], and sensors [14]. In addition, the functionalization of the coupling of N, S co-doped GQDs with other materials was also explored [15–17]. The above analysis demonstrated that selecting appropriate precursors and controlling preparation conditions were critical for fabricating doped GQDs with superior properties, which in turn expanded their potential applications.

In view of the advantages of GQDs and modification method, a more efficient, simpler, and cheaper method for large-scale preparation of GQDs was urgently needed. The GQDs, as small-sized two-dimensional materials, could be categorized into few-layer and multi-layer structures. Notably, the number of layers and the interlayer twist angle significantly influence the properties of such two-dimensional materials. For example, the twisted bilayer graphene exhibited the unique insulating and superconducting states compared with the traditional structure of graphene [18,19]. The single-layer or few-layer two-dimensional materials possessed the unique excellent advantages [20–22]. Furthermore, compared with the multi-layer GQDs, the few-layer GQDs was not only had excellent optical properties, but also more conducive to self-assembly because of the lower steric resistance between layers. So far, only a few studies investigated single-layer GQDs and its application for biology [23–25], sensing [26–28], and light emitting devices [29,30]. Although single-layer GQDs prepared by various methods demonstrate diverse performance attributed, they often suffered from defects arising during synthesis and in their resulting properties. For examples, the preparation process often involved the use of strong acids [24,25,29], while the template method was complex and difficult to operate [26–28,30]. Additionally, the synthesized single-layer GQDs typically required low-temperature storage [23,28], and most of them still display excitation-independent properties [26,28,30]. The excitation-dependent property allowed for easily regulated fluorescence emission by simply changing the excitation wavelength without altering the structure. The excitation-dependent emission was explained via variety of models and mechanisms including quantum confined effect, surface trap model, giant red edge effect, edge state model, and synergistic effect [31,32]. In addition, the redshift of emission peak with excitation-dependence was always accompanied by a decrease in fluorescence intensity. It was interesting that the multicolor fluorescence emission could be induced by concentration [33], which was an effective method to achieve multi-color emission and ideal fluorescence intensity by changing the concentration. The establishment of a reliable method to produce few-layer GQDs with concentration- and excitation-dependent properties would greatly broaden their applicability across various fields.

In this study, N, S-doped GQDs was prepared by using simple solvothermal method with 2, 2-dithiobenzoic acid and o-phenylenediamine. The structure and morphology of prepared GQDs were analyzed by means of characterizations. And the photo-stability, excitation and solution dependence were explored. At last the GQDs were successfully used to build white light emitting devices (WLED). This study not only provided a simple and convenient method for preparing multiple adjustable fluorescent GQDs, but also demonstrated the excellent potential for constructing WLED.

The O-phenylenediamine and 2, 2-dithiodibenzoic acid were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. The ethanol (EtOH), toluene, dichloromethane (DCM), ethyl acrylate (EA), acetone, and acetonitrile (ACN) were acquired from the China National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The methyl triethoxysilane (MTES) and acetic acid were purchased from Beijing Innokai Technology Co., Ltd. The other reagents were analytically pure. Unless stated otherwise, the used water was deionized.

The GQDs was prepared via solvothermal method [34]. In detail, O-phenylenediamine and 2, 2-dithiodibenzoic acid were dissolved in EtOH under ultrasonic conditions. The solution was transferred to high-pressure reactor which was kept at 180°C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reactant was centrifuged (10000 rpm 10 min) and filtered (0.22 μm filter) to remove large particles. And then, the solution was further purified via 1000 Da dialysis bag. Finally, the purified solution was steamed and dried to obtain N, S-doped few-layer GQDs, named as Y-GQDs. The process was provided in Fig. 1. To analyze the effects of functional groups on PL intensity, the 2, 2-dithiobenzoic acid or o-phenylenediamine were used as the precursor to obtain GQDs at the same conditions.

MTES of 20 mL was dissolved in EtOH under magnetic stirring. And the solution with 6.5 mL deionized water, 35.25 mL EtOH, and 0.25 mL acetic acid was slowly added to MTES solution. The above mixture was stirred for 24 h and then rotated to remove half the volume of solvent, and then stirred at room temperature for another week to get MTES Gel solution.

The Y-GQDs were added into 8.8 mL MTES Gel solution and mixed well. The suspension was vacuum dried to lose fluidity. And then, it was coated on the blue LED chip and placed in the oven at 50°C for drying and curing to obtain WLED. For comparison of luminous characteristics, the MTES Gel solution with yellow phosphor YAG:Ce material was as the luminophor, and other operations were similar to the above process.

Morphological images of high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TME, JEM-2100F) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Dimension Fastscan Bio.) were observed. The crystalline structures were detected via X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker-D8-Focus diffractometer with (Cu Kα irradiation λ = 0.15405 nm)). Furthermore, the functional groups on the surface of prepared GQDs were characterized via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, EscaLab 250Xi) and fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Excalibur 3100). The optical absorption characteristics were studied using ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometer (Pgeneral, T1800). Time-resolved photoluminescence lifetime measurements were performed using Transient/steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000).

The morphology and lattice size of prepared Y-GQDs sample were characterized via AFM and HRTEM in Fig. 2 and Fig. S1 in Supplementary materials. The sample distribution was uniform and sphere-like morphology (Fig. 2(a) and Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. 2(b), the lattice spacing was approximately 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [35,36]. And the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern was shown in Fig. 2(c), indicating that the prepared Y-GQDs possessed single-crystal structure, and the results were consistent with previous studies [37,38]. Fig. 2(d) illustrated the diameter distribution of the Y-GQDs sample, showing mean size of 2.8 nm and standard deviation of 0.6 nm. The above results approved that the prepared Y-GQDs with excellent dispersity and narrow size distribution was achieved successfully. The AFM could be a good analysis of the thickness of the GQDs. The topographic heights of prepared Y-GQDs were range from 0.6 to 0.8 nm (Fig. 2(e and f)). Dai's study [36] approved that the obtained CDs was below 1 nm which was equal to 1–2 graphene layers. Based on above conclusion, the prepared Y-GQDs had a few-layer structure and most of them were single-layer structure [37,39].

The XRD patterns showed a bulge (002) peak centered at around 23.6° (Fig. 2(e)), indicating that graphene structure of Y-GQDs with high crystallinity [40,41], which were consistent with the result of SAED analysis. The Raman spectrum approved that prepared Y-GQDs possessed high degree of graphitization (Fig. 2(h)). The crystalline G band and D band were at 1588 cm−1 and 1344 cm−1 corresponding to the in-plane stretching vibration of sp
2 hybrid carbon atom and defects in the lattice of carbon atoms, respectively [39]. And the G band was much stronger than D band, and the ID/IG was about 0.78, which was consistent with the result of AFM and HRTEM images. Furthermore, the 2D band was located at about 2700 cm−1, and the width of 2D band was large than that of G band. However, the structure of 2D band was no apparent symmetry, indicating that the Y-GQDs possessed the structure with different layers, which were consistent with the AFM analysis. The above results approved that the prepared GQDs possessed with few-layers high crystallinity graphene structure.

Surface structure properties played important roles in luminescence of Y-GQDs. Hence, the surface functional groups of Y-GQDs were detected by the FTIR spectrum ( Fig. 3(a)) and XPS spectra (Fig. 3(b-f)). In the FTIR spectrum, the peaks of 3425 cm−1, 2973 cm−1 and 2868 cm−1, 2656 cm−1, and 1680 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of primary amine and hydroxyl group, methyl and methylene group, S-H, and CN, respectively. Also, the peaks located at 1628 cm−1 and 1591 cm−1 confirmed that the amido bond was produced. In addition, the peaks of 1464 cm−1, 1260 cm−1, 1100 cm−1, 1000 cm−1, 685 cm−1, and 490 cm−1 belonged to stretching vibration of CC, C-NH, C-O, CO (S), C-S, and S-S, respectively [41,42], indicating that Y-GQDs contained rich nitrogen and sulfur functional groups. The above results demonstrated that the surface of few-layer GQDs contained various functional groups, including hydroxyl, methyl, methylene, carbonyl, and carboxyl, contributing to their versatility in functional applications. The XPS survey suggested that Y-GQDs were composed of C, O, N, and S atoms (Fig. 3(b)) and their contents were shown in Table S1. The N and S contents in sample were 1.41 % and 1.54 %, respectively, which confirmed the analysis of FTIR. In detail, the C 1s high-resolution spectra could be de-convoluted into three peaks corresponding to C-C/CC (284.8 eV), CO/CN (286.4 eV), and C-N (288.8 eV) [43]. The deconvolution of the N 1s revealed three distinct peaks corresponding to pyrrole nitrogen (401.3 eV), amino nitrogen (400.3 eV), and pyridine nitrogen (398.6 eV) [41], confirming the successful N doping of the GQDs and the presence of abundant nitrogen-containing functional groups. And the O 1s was de-convoluted into C-O (533.3 eV) and CO (531.8 eV) [44]. The S 2p could be de-convoluted into five peaks which located at 163.4 eV, 163.8 eV, 164.7 eV, 168.6 eV and 170.0 eV, corresponding to S-C, S-H, S-S, -C-SO3-C-, and -C-SO4-C-, respectively [45], indicating that the surface of Y-GQDs was modified by sulfur functional groups. The various functional groups and contents were provided in Table S2. Based on the XPS and FTIR analysis, the Y-GQDs possessed a variety of functional groups with N and S, which approved that the O-phenylenediamine and 2, 2-dithiodibenzoic acid as the precursors experiences via carbonization and polymerization etc. to generate the expected N and S co-doped GQDs.

Importantly, the few-layer structure of GQDs was conducive to the self-assembly compared with the multiple layers of GQDs, due to the low steric hindrance between layers of single-layer GQDs. In addition, doping heteroatoms could generate rich functional groups, which was conducive to the further coupling of GQDs. Hence, the prepared N, S-doped GQD helped the self-assembly process, which could further improve the photo-electric properties.

The optical properties of Y-GQDs, as a key physical characteristic determining their potential for practical applications, were thoroughly investigated. The Y-GQDs were dispersed in ETOH to invesgate optical property via UV–vis, photoluminescence excitation, and photoluminescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the absorption spectrum of UV–vis showed strong absorption in the ultraviolet region due to the π-π* transition of CC, and the absorption peaks at 383 nm and 430 nm were attributed to the n-π* transition of CN/CO, C-O, C-S [46]. In detail, the obvious transfer of energy between the two peaks was caused by localized defect level or non-bonding orbitals of surface sites [47,48]. Also, these absorption bands could be found in excitation spectrum, and the strong excitation signals were located at 440 nm and 381 nm. As for the photoluminescence spectrum, the optimal excitation wavelength of Y-GQDs was 380 nm, and the emission spectrum showed green light at 535 nm, indicated that the photo-excited electrons could transfer from surface C-O energy levels to CO surface traps through internal conversion [49]. According to the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) color coordinates (0.35, 0.57) analysis, the fluorescence of Y-GQDs was yellow-green (Fig. 4(b)). And the absolute fluorescence quantum efficiency of prepared Y-GQDs was 10.77 %. For comparison of PL properties, the YAG:Ce material-MTES Gel film was prepared. And the PL properties were shown in Fig. S2, the excitation signals of the film was located at 457 nm and 339 nm. When the excitation wavelength was 450 nm, the fluorescence spectrum showed yellow-green emission at 575 nm. In addition, the PL intensities of prepared GQDs with 2, 2-dithiobenzoic acid or o-phenylenediamine as raw materials were shown in Fig. S3 and Fig. S4. The optimal excitation wavelengths were 356 nm and 382 nm, and the emission spectra centered at 421 nm and 554 nm, respectively, which was obviously different from the Y-GQDs. The functional groups including -C-SO3-C-, -C-SO4-C-, C-N would be generated in the process of pyrolysis polymerization, thereby leading to the generation of yellow-green emission [36]. The Y-GQDs was dissolved in ethanol and irradiated continuously under a 365 nm UV lamp for one hour to detect their photo-stability. The fluorescence spectrum was measured every ten minutes, as shown in Fig. S5(a) and Fig. 4(c). The fluorescence peaks and intensities did not change significantly, indicating that Y-GQDs had good photo-stability and photo-bleaching resistance under UV light. Y-GQDs were left at room temperature for 30 days and the fluorescence spectrum was measured at intervals. Within 30 days, the Y-GQDs showed exceptional fluorescence stability, with no shift in the emission peak position and only negligible reduction in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S5(b) and Fig. 4(d)). The superior stability of Y-GQDs could be attributed to the effective surface modification achieved through co-doping with N and S elements.

The fluorescence emissions demonstrated distinct excitation-dependent behavior, exhibiting a continuous red-shift in the emission peak with increasing excitation wavelength, which resulted in a visible color transition from yellow-green to orange-yellow, as confirmed by the CIE chromaticity coordinates ( Fig. 5(a and b)). In details, when the excitation wavelength increased from 380 nm to 480 nm, the fluorescence peaks only showed a slight redshift from 535 nm to 543 nm. And when it continued to increase from 480 nm to 510 nm, the fluorescence peaks redshift increased from 543 nm to 586 nm. The fluorescence peak red-shift gradually stabilized as the excitation wavelength increased, reaching a plateau at 590 nm, while simultaneously giving rise to a secondary emission peak at 645 nm. The prepared Y-GQDs exhibited excitation dependence, suggesting the presence of various emission sites and that their luminescent properties were potentially governed by their intrinsic states [50,51]. The diverse functional groups including CO/CN, S-C, S-H, S-S, -C-SO3-C-, and -C-SO4-C-, presented on the surface of the Y-GQDs introduce a series of energy levels between the π-π* states, resulting in various emission sites. Hence, at a specific excitation wavelength, one emission site would be dominant, and as the excitation wavelength changes, the corresponding emission site would become dominant [32]. To approve the phenomenon, the fluorescence lifetimes τ of Y-GQDs under three excitation wavelengths including 375 nm, 450 nm, and 510 nm were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 5(d) and Table S3, the fluorescence lifetimes of Y-GQDs conformed to the three-exponential fitting [31]. As the excitation wavelengths increased, the fluorescence lifetimes decreased from 8.07 ns to 3.22 ns, confirming the presence of three emission sites in Y-GQDs at 535 nm, 590 nm, and 645 nm. Alongside the characteristic red-shifting behavior, the system exhibited pronounced excitation-wavelength-dependent intensity variations in its fluorescence emission, as shown in Fig. 5(c) (the normalized fluorescence spectra). At the optimal excitation wavelength of 380 nm, the fluorescence intensity showed the strongest. When the excitation wavelength increased from 380 nm to 430 nm, the fluorescence intensity continued to decline. Interestingly, when the excitation wavelength increased from 430 nm to 460 nm, the fluorescence intensity increased, which was caused by the absorption of Y-GQDs at 430 nm corresponding to UV–vis and photoluminescence excitation spectra (Fig. 4(a)). After excitation wavelength 460 nm, the fluorescence intensity still decreased.

To analyze solvent dependence, the Y-GQDs were dispersed in eight different organic solvents (toluene, methylene chloride, ethyl acetate, acetone, acetonitrile, DMF, ethanol, and methanol) at concentration of 1 mg/mL, and the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured. The solutions presented a characteristic color dichotomy: yellowish in visible light and yellow-green luminescent under UV irradiation (Fig. S6). In detail, at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm, the fluorescence peaks of Y-GQDs varied in different solvents ( Fig. 6(a and c)). With increase of solvent polarity, the fluorescence peak gradually redshifts from 524 nm in toluene to 542 nm in methanol. And the highest fluorescence intensity was obtained in methanol. The main UV–vis absorption peaks of Y-GQDs in solvent also showed a similar phenomenon (Fig. 6(b and d)). And the red shift was from 415 nm to 429 nm with the increase of solvent polarity. Furthermore, the influence of solvent effect on energy band was analyzed. According to the Eg=hc/λAbs= 1240/λAbs [52], with increase of solvent polarity, the absorption peak redshifted and the energy level band gap decreased, which led to the redshift of fluorescence peak. The comprehensive analysis confirmed that Y-GQDs display pronounced solvent-sensitive behavior, largely determined by the polarity parameters of solvent. Therefore, the solvent could be adjusted as needed to obtain the Y-GQDs solution with the desired fluorescence emission, thus meeting the needs of more applications. To further explore its application, the excitation behaviors in different solvents were detected. As shown in Fig. S7, the tunability of fluorescence properties was constrained within a relatively narrow range. Although, the Y-GQDs exhibited similar excitation dependent behavior in different solvents, the optimal excitation wavelength varied slightly, which could effective broaden their scope of applications.

For systematic examination of concentration-influenced fluorescence behavior, multiple Y-GQD ethanol solutions with precisely controlled concentration were fabricated. Fig. S8 showed the corresponding photographs under daylight and ultraviolet light. Under daylight, the color of the solution gradually deepens as the concentration of Y-GQDs increased. And Y-GQDs solutions of different concentrations exhibited different fluorescence emission from cyan to yellow under ultraviolet light. For the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 6(e)), as the concentrations of Y-GQDs increased, the intensity of peaks increased. In addition, when the concentrations of Y-GQDs were above 1 mg/mL, the position of adsorption peak was redshifted. Fig. 6(f) showed the fluorescence spectra of Y-GQDs solutions at different concentrations (low and high). In details, at lower concentrations (below 1 mg/mL), Y-GQDs did not show significant concentration dependence. However, when the Y-GQDs concentration increased from 1 mg/mL to 12 mg/mL, the maximum emission wavelength increased from 535 nm to 593 nm. The reasons for the above phenomena were attributed to the followed reasons. (a) With increasing concentration, the N- and S-rich surface functional groups of Y-GQDs promoted extensive hydrogen bonding [53]. (b) The few-layer structure of Y-GQDs, combined with intergroup interactions, facilitated self-assembly into aggregated clusters, exhibiting a reduced band gap and consequently leading to a red-shift in emission wavelength [54]. The fluorescence intensity of different Y-GQDs concentrations were analyzed in detail (Fig. 6(g and h)). The fluorescence intensity of Y-GQDs at different concentrations increased first and then decreased. In detail, as the concentration increases from 0.1 mg/mL to 1 mg/mL, the fluorescence intensity increased, indicating that the aggregation of Y-GQDs generated mutually oscillating conjugated aromatic rings, and the non-radiative complexation process of Y-GQDs decreased with the consumption of excited electrons [33]. And with increase in concentration (from 1 mg/mL to 12 mg/mL), Y-GQDs molecules came into close contact and formed more aggregates, which enhanced the non-radiative relaxation process and closed the radiative channel, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity [55]. Moreover, the Y-GQDs exhibited significant stimulation-dependent behavior regardless of concentration (Fig. S9). When the concentration of Y-GQDs was below 1 mg/mL, the optimal excitation wavelength was located at 380 nm. However, when the concentration of Y-GQDs was higher than 1 mg/mL, the optimal excitation wavelength gradually redshifted with increasing concentration. And at high concentrations, the fluorescence intensity at low excitation wavelengths was very low. The prepared Y-GQDs not only exhibited excitation dependent and solvent dependent emission, but also exhibited concentration dependent emission characteristics, which was of great significance for application of Y-GQDs in optoelectronic devices.

Due to its unique optical properties and excellent stability, Y-GQDs could be used to prepare WLED. When the doping concentration was 3 %wt, the prepared LEDs could emit bright white light at 3 V driving voltage and 50 mA driving current ( Fig. 7(a)), and the external quantum efficiency (EQE, %) was 3622.29. The larger the EQE, the more photons were emitted to the outside, indicating that the prepared WLED was very bright. The WLED manufactured by Y-GQDs and Blu-ray chip corresponded to the color coordinate of CIE (0.33, 0.32) (Fig. 7(b)), which was very close to the color coordinate of positive white light (0.33, 0.33). Furthermore, at the same condition, the luminous efficiency of Y-GQDs based WLED was 9693.48 lm/W, which was higher than that of the yellow phosphor YAG:Ce material based WLED (4596.36 lm/W, EQE (%) 1647.79). The result of luminous efficiency was consistent with those of the EQE, which was further confirmed that the prepared WLED possessed very bright luminescence characteristics. Fig. 7(c) showed the electroluminescence spectra of the WLED at different driving currents from 5 to 300 mA, and the luminous intensity of white LEDs also increased with increase of the driving current. The driving current passed through the LEDs, which led to the electron holes recombine, and then releasing energy into light energy. Enhanced driving currents increased both the electron-hole recombination frequency and the resulting luminescence intensity. [56]. Fig. 7(d) showed the variation curves of color rendering index and color temperature at different driving currents. When the driving current increased from 5 mA to 100 mA, the color temperature decreased from 5541 K to 5267 K. However, when the driving current was increased from 100 mA to 300 mA, the color temperature was increased from 5267 K to 5498 K. Furthermore, the color rendering index exhibited enhancement from 82.6 to 85.1 with increasing driving current. The detailed data of WLED parameters including color coordinates, color rendering index, and color temperature, were shown in Table 1. In general, with increase of driving current, the changes of luminous intensity, color coordinate, color temperature and color rendering index of WLED were not obvious, which indicated that the prepared WLED possessed good light stability under different current driving. The obtained WLED with N, S-doped GQDs met the conditions for commercial use compared with the commercial WLED. In addition, the fabricated WLED exhibited optimal photometric parameters with color coordinates of (0.3364, 0.3270), correlated color temperature of 5306 K, and color rendering index of 83.7. The performance of the WLED was compared with other studies and the results were shown in Table 2. To analysis the thermal quenching properties of the prepared WLED, the WLED was quenched at different temperatures. And the electroluminescence spectra were shown in Fig. S10. After reaction, the values of EQE and luminous efficiency were not significantly changed, which indicated that the prepared WLED excellent thermal quenching properties. In addition, our previous study [57] showed that the lack of CD-Gels film weight was less than 5 % via the TGA curves analysis of CD-Gels at heating rate 10 ºC min−1 to 900 ºC min−1 under N2. Based on the above results, the prepared the N, S-doped GQDs possessed the excellent photo-stability. Hence, the WLED prepared based on the N, S-doped GQDs had the application potential as a high performance lighting source.

In this study, the N, S-doped GQDs with abundant surface groups were successfully synthesized by solvothermal method. The competition of multiple fluorescent centers caused the excitation dependence, and the emission wavelength redshifts with the increase of excitation wavelength. In addition, the increased polarity of solvent and the aggregation of Y-GQDs could decrease the energy level band gap, which led to the redshift of fluorescence peak. The Y-GQDs-prepared WLED successfully achieved a color rendering index of 83.7, a color temperature of 5306 K, and exhibited excellent photo-stability via different driving currents. The development of green and environmentally friendly preparation technology for GQDs, leveraging the advantages of 2D materials, was crucial for their future application, and the study of their properties could further broaden their application field.

Lv Xugui: Investigation. Li Nan: Methodology, Investigation. Xie Zheng: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ma Qingyu: Project administration. Wang Jingjing: Investigation. Liu Xinghao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22275202, 21875267), University of Jinan Disciplinary Cross-Convergence Construction Project 2023(XKJC-202312) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2024QD168) were acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2025.179827.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Du, J. Zheng, J. Wang, Y. Yang, X. Liu, The synthesis of green fluorescent carbon dots for warm white LEDs, RSC Adv., 8 (2018) 19585-19595.
